title: How to Negotiate a Rent-Free Period on a Commercial Lease
metaTitle: How to Negotiate a Rent-Free Period on a Commercial Lease | Your Office Space
metaDescription: Rent-free periods of 3–6 months are common in commercial leases. Here's how to negotiate one, how long to expect, and when to push for more.
excerpt: A rent-free period is one of the most valuable lease incentives available — and most tenants leave it on the table. Here's how to negotiate one.
author: Joe Kelley
body:
## What is a rent-free period?

A rent-free period is a block of time at the start of a commercial lease where the tenant pays no rent. The space is theirs — they can fit it out and operate from it — but the clock on rent doesn't start until the rent-free period ends. On a standard 3–5 year lease, a rent-free period of 3–6 months is achievable in most Australian markets.

## Why landlords offer rent-free periods

Landlords offer rent-free periods because vacant space costs them money too. Every month the tenancy sits empty, the landlord is still paying outgoings, body corporate fees, and loan costs with no income to offset them.

A good tenant on a long lease is worth more to a landlord than holding out for face rent. Offering a rent-free period is how they bridge the gap between what they're asking and what the market will pay.

This means rent-free periods are not charity — they're a negotiating tool that benefits both sides.

## How long can you get?

The length of a rent-free period depends on:

- **Lease term** — longer leases attract more rent-free
- **Market conditions** — high vacancy gives tenants more leverage
- **Asset class** — premium office buildings tend to offer more; secondary stock less
- **Your covenant** — strong, creditworthy tenants get better terms
- **Fitout works required** — if you need to gut the space, a landlord will factor in construction time

| Market Conditions | Typical Rent-Free (3yr lease) | Typical Rent-Free (5yr lease) |
|---|---|---|
| Tenant's market (high vacancy) | 3–6 months | 6–9 months |
| Balanced market | 1–3 months | 3–6 months |
| Landlord's market (low vacancy) | 0–1 month | 1–3 months |
| Premium / Trophy asset | 0–1 month | 1–2 months |

These are guides. The actual result depends on how well you negotiate.

## Negotiation tactics that work

### Start early

If you're renewing a lease, start 12 months before expiry. If you're looking for new space, give yourself at least 6 months. Rushed tenants get poor deals. A landlord knows when you're desperate.

### Know the market before you ask

Don't walk into a negotiation blind. Know what comparable spaces are leasing for, how long they've been on the market, and what incentives are being offered elsewhere. Your ask needs to be grounded in real data.

### Create competition

Landlords respond to competition. If you're looking at multiple properties, let each landlord know. You don't need to be aggressive about it — just be clear that you have options. That changes the dynamic.

### Ask for more than you expect to get

If you want 3 months rent-free, open at 5. You'll meet somewhere in the middle. If you open at 3, you might land at 1.

### Tie it to the fitout period

If your fitout takes 6 weeks, that's 6 weeks where the space is a construction site. Ask for at least that period as rent-free. It's hard for a landlord to argue you should pay full rent while their building is full of tradespeople.

### Get it in the Heads of Agreement

Rent-free periods need to be agreed in the Heads of Agreement before the formal lease is drafted. If it's not in the HoA, it's easy for the landlord to walk it back.

## When to push harder

Push for more rent-free when:

- The space has been vacant for more than 3 months
- The landlord is carrying the fitout cost but you're doing the work
- You're committing to a longer term (5+ years)
- You're a strong tenant (established business, good financials, known brand)
- You're taking a larger space than the landlord expected to fill

## The real value of rent-free

On a 500 sqm space at $380/sqm, three months rent-free is worth $47,500. That's real money — and it doesn't show up on the face rent. It's one reason net effective rent matters more than the headline number.
